argumentCard.centralQuestion: What is the proper boundary between individual freedom and society's authority to coerce?
argumentCard.thesis: The only legitimate reason for society to restrict individual liberty — whether through law or social pressure — is to prevent harm to others. Liberty of thought, expression, and lifestyle must be nearly absolute, because diversity of opinion and ways of life is the engine of human progress.
keyMoves: Establishes the Harm Principle: power may only be exercised over an individual to prevent harm to others, never for their own good or to enforce majority opinion
Defends absolute freedom of thought and expression on the grounds that even false opinions sharpen our grasp of truth, and unchallenged truths become dead dogma
Argues for 'experiments in living' — the freedom to pursue radically different lifestyles — as the only way societies discover better ways of being human
Warns that the tyranny of prevailing opinion can be more oppressive than legal coercion, and that democratic majorities are no guarantee of individual liberty
argumentCard.strongestObjection: The Harm Principle is far harder to apply than it appears: almost any action can be argued to harm someone, and Mill's own expansive vision of 'higher pleasures' smuggles in a substantive view of the good life that undermines the neutrality the principle promises.

debates.title: Is democracy's greatest threat the government — or the crowd?
debates.subtitle: Mill's harm principle vs. Tocqueville's warning about soft despotism

philosopher1.name: John Stuart Mill
philosopher1.position: Individual liberty must be near-absolute — only harm to others justifies restriction
philosopher1.keyQuote: The only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilized community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others.
arguments: The greatest danger to liberty comes from legal coercion — the state using force to suppress opinions and ways of life it dislikes. This must be resisted absolutely.
Free expression is essential not only for individuals but for society: false opinions are overthrown by free debate; true ones are kept alive rather than becoming dead dogma.
Experiments in living — allowing individuals to pursue their own vision of the good life — generate the diversity that makes social progress possible.

philosopher2.name: Alexis de Tocqueville
philosopher2.position: Democracy generates a 'soft despotism' more insidious than government tyranny
philosopher2.keyQuote: It does not tyrannize, it hinders, it represses, it enervates, it extinguishes, it stupefies, and finally reduces each nation to a herd of timid and industrious animals.
arguments: The real threat in democratic societies is not the state but social conformity — the tyranny of majority opinion that makes individuals afraid to think differently.
Democratic citizens, absorbed in private comfort, become passive and incapable of self-governance. They outsource their thinking to the crowd and their governance to administrators.
Mill focuses on legal liberty, but Tocqueville sees the deeper problem: a society where everyone thinks alike and innovation is socially punished is already unfree, even without laws.

debates.stakes: Both thinkers were worried about the same thing — freedom disappearing from democratic societies — but they disagreed about where the threat originates. Their debate shapes contemporary worries about political correctness, cancel culture, and technocratic governance.
debates.yourTurn: Which threat feels more real to you today — government overreach or social conformity? Is it possible to address both simultaneously, or does focusing on one make the other worse?
